perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party by giving thirty (30) days notice in writing to the other party without assigning any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party by giving ten (10) days notice in writing to the other party without assigning any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination to 10 days could be problematic if the agreement falls under regulations requiring longer notice, especially considering the parties are located in different countries (India and US).
perturbation.law_explanation: Although M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 doesn't specify a minimum notice period for contract termination, it broadly prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in business. Terminating a potentially complex business relationship with only 10 days' notice, especially if it causes significant harm to the other party, could be argued as an unfair practice, especially if the original 30 day period was considered a necessary buffer. The 10 day period could be seen as unconscionably short.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period from 30 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that this reduction "could be argued as an unfair practice, especially if the original 30 day period was considered a necessary buffer." The explanation ties this to M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2, which the scraped snippets confirm declares "unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful." Reducing the notice period to such a degree introduces an inconsistency that could contradict the law if a court deems it an unfair business practice, creating an enforceable and serious contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11. If any dispute arises between the parties hereto during the subsistence or thereafter, in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, the dispute shall be referred to arbitration under the Indian Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1995 by a panel of three arbitrators. Each party will appoint one arbitrator and the two arbitrators so appointed will appoint the third or the presiding arbitrator. Arbitration shall be held in Hyderabad, India. The proceedings of arbitration shall be in the English language. The arbitrator’s award shall be final and binding on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. If any dispute arises between the parties hereto during the subsistence or thereafter, in connection with or arising out of this Agreement, the dispute shall be resolved solely through private negotiation. Neither party may pursue legal action in any jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing dispute resolution solely through private negotiation and preventing legal action, might be seen as an unconscionable and unenforceable waiver of rights. Massachusetts has laws governing arbitration and court access.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 251, § 1 establishes the validity and enforceability of arbitration agreements. While parties can agree to arbitration, completely barring access to courts for any dispute resolution may be against public policy, especially if arbitration is not a viable or fair option. The new text removes any possibility of arbitration which is itself a protected legal mechanism.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part III, Title IV, Chapter 251, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part III COURTS, JUDICIAL OFFICERS AND PROCEEDINGS IN CIVIL CASES Title IV CERTAIN WRITS AND PROCEEDINGS IN SPECIAL CASES Chapter 251 UNIFORM ARBITRATION ACT FOR COMMERCIAL DISPUTES Section 1 Validity of agreements; non-applicability to collective bargaining agreements Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Validity of agreements; non-applicability to collective bargaining agreements Section 1. A written agreement to submit any existing controversy to arbitration or a provision in a written contract to submit to arbitration any controversy thereafter arising between the parties shall be valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. The provisions of this chapter shall not apply to collective bargaining agreements to arbitrate, which are subject to the provisions of chapter one hundred and fifty C, except as provided by the provisions of chapter one hundred and fifty-two. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the possibility of arbitration and bars legal action, directly contradicting M.G.L. c. 251, § 1, as indicated in both scraped law snippets. These snippets explicitly state that agreements to submit controversies to arbitration are valid, enforceable, and irrevocable, 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract.' The altered contract removes this enforceable right, establishing a clear contradiction as it prevents access to a protected legal mechanism.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain valid for a period of three (3) years from the date of execution of this Agreement which term may be extended by mutual consent in writing of both the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain valid indefinitely from the date of execution of this Agreement and cannot be terminated.
perturbation.explanation: Making the agreement perpetually valid and non-terminable is problematic, especially considering the complexities of international business relationships and Massachusetts contract law. This might be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93, § 1 addresses contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. A perpetual, non-terminable agreement could be construed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it prevents BCGI (the Massachusetts company) from engaging in similar or competing business ventures in the future. While the law focuses on preventing monopolies and cartels, the principle of allowing businesses to operate freely informs the interpretation of contractual limitations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 1 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 1 Massachusetts Antitrust Act; short title applicable to Secs. 1 to 14A; purpose Section 1 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 1: Massachusetts Antitrust Act; short title applicable to Secs. 1 to 14A; purpose Section 1. Sections one to fourteen A, inclusive, shall be known, and may be cited, as the ''Massachusetts Antitrust Act''. It is the purpose of this chapter to encourage free and open competition in the interests of the general welfare and economy by prohibiting unreasonable restraints of trade and monopolistic practices in the commonwealth. This chapter shall be construed in harmony with judicial interpretations of comparable federal antitrust statutes insofar as practicable. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The changed text makes the agreement perpetually valid and non-terminable, while the original text allowed for termination by mutual consent after three years. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that such a perpetual agreement may be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, potentially conflicting with M.G.L. c. 93, § 1. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 state that the purpose of Chapter 93 is to encourage free and open competition by prohibiting unreasonable restraints of trade and monopolistic practices. Therefore, the modified contract text contradicts the law by potentially creating an unreasonable restraint of trade through its perpetual and non-terminable nature.

perturbation.original_text: 14. This Agreement shall remain valid for a period of three (3) years from the date of execution of this Agreement which term may be extended by mutual consent in writing of both the parties. This Agreement may be terminated by either party by giving thirty (30) days notice in writing to the other party without assigning any reason whatsoever. The obligations of each party hereunder will continue and be binding irrespective of whether the discussion between the parties materialise into a specific understanding/business relationship or not and for a further period of three (3) years after termination/expiry of the Agreement. In any event, however, the obligations of each Party to maintain the confidentiality of the Confidential Information it has received under this agreement shall continue indefinitely as to Confidential Information constituting a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. This Agreement shall remain valid for a period of one (1) year from the date of execution of this Agreement which term cannot be extended. This Agreement may be terminated by XIUS at any time, without notice, and without assigning any reason whatsoever. BCGI's obligations will continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Creating a situation where one party (XIUS, the Indian company) can terminate the agreement at any time without notice, while the other party (BCGI, a Massachusetts company) remains bound indefinitely, is likely unconscionable under Massachusetts law due to the extreme imbalance of power and obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: While M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 generally prohibits unfair and deceptive business practices, this change introduces a one-sided termination clause that gives all the power to one party and leaves the other exposed indefinitely. This would likely be viewed as an unfair business practice that violates the spirit and intent of the law. Massachusetts courts are hesitant to enforce contracts with wildly skewed obligations and termination rights.

perturbation.justification: The modification creates a significant imbalance in the agreement, granting XIUS the right to terminate at any time without notice while binding BCGI indefinitely. This directly contradicts the principle of fair business practices as outlined in Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 2, which declares "Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful." The altered contract terms appear to be unfair and deceptive, vesting excessive power in one party and disproportionately burdening the other, thus violating the spirit of the law.
